target.name: Re:Build Manufacturing
target.founder: Miles Arnone, Jeff Wilke, Anthony Manzo, Michael Foley

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $545M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Robotics & Manufacturing (found 0 same-stage, 2 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 7 same-sector peers exist in Robotics & Manufacturing, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: SendCutSend
private.rationale: Same sector (Robotics & Manufacturing), same stage (Series A)

data.narrative:
Re:Build Manufacturing, founded by Miles Arnone, Jeff Wilke, Anthony Manzo, and Michael Foley, is a Series A robotics and manufacturing company that has raised $545M to date. The sector comprises 9 comparable companies—1 public (277810.KQ) and 8 private peers including Divergent, Exotec, Locus Robotics, Bedrock Robotics, and Dexterity—though same-stage and adjacent-stage clustering within this universe remains sparse.

Of the two valuation methods assessed, only stage-progression proved applicable. This approach uses the $545M total raised as a proxy for current enterprise value and applies a 2.0x multiplier typical of Series A-to-next-stage step-ups, yielding an implied valuation of $1.1B. The peer-median method was inapplicable due to insufficient narrow peer density; while 7 same-sector peers exist, fewer than 3 share matching stage, adjacency, or valuation tier, making sector-wide median comparison misleading.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $926M (low) to $1.3B (high), with a median point of $1.1B. This range is materially constrained by reliance on a single method and the thinness of directly comparable stage and sector peers. Key uncertainties include the appropriate step-up multiple for Re:Build's specific market position, technology maturity, and capital intensity relative to the broader robotics sector. Additional disclosure of revenue, unit economics, or deployment metrics would strengthen conviction.
